issue.title: W25: intelligence needs a source-backed operating file.
issue.dek: A controlled W25 draft built from source-backed candidates across AI governance, medical devices, quality systems, clinical operations, market access, health data, and biopharma AI.
issue.lede: This W25 draft replaces the earlier placeholder records with source-backed candidates. It remains a test-mode, noindex issue until final editorial selection, source check, claim check, CMS handoff, domain confirmation, and publish approval are complete.

workflow.notes: Source-backed controlled draft imported from the iFeed Master Platform W25 source register on 2026-06-18. Keep testMode true until human/editor approval and deployment target confirmation.

signals.title: High-risk AI classification is becoming operating architecture.
signals.cardFact: AI Act classification work is moving from policy language into source-backed records about intended purpose, risk route, owner review, and claim boundary.
signals.whyItMatters: Classification decisions become operational only when teams can show how the source was read, what the AI system does, and why the conclusion was reached.
signals.sourceTitle: Draft Commission guidelines on classification of high-risk AI systems
signals.expandedSummary: The European Commission draft high-risk AI classification source gives iFeed a clear governance signal: classification cannot sit as a vague legal label. It needs an operating record that connects intended purpose, product or safety-component relationship, source interpretation, reviewer, and limitation. For iFeed, the important move is not to overstate the consultation as final guidance, but to turn it into a controlled decision workflow.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: AI Act classification affects what evidence, controls, and accountability records a team needs.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: AI governance, regulatory, quality, product, clinical safety, and legal review teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Create a classification record with source, intended purpose, risk route, reviewer, and claim boundary.

visual.title: Classification becomes a record
visual.footer: Do not publish the interpretation without separating source fact from iFeed view.

signals.claimBoundary: Draft consultation source only. This is not legal advice and should not be framed as final Commission guidance.

signals.title: AI-enabled device evidence is now a visible market map.
signals.cardFact: FDA's public AI/ML-enabled medical device list makes the regulated AI-device landscape inspectable by device area, date, specialty, and source record.
signals.whyItMatters: A public list turns AI-device intelligence from broad trend language into a traceable market and evidence map.
signals.sourceTitle: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AI/ML)-Enabled Medical Devices
signals.expandedSummary: The FDA AI/ML-enabled medical devices page is useful because it gives operators a source-backed way to inspect the AI-device landscape rather than describe it generally. For iFeed, the signal is that MedTech AI content should not just say that AI devices are increasing. It should connect public device records to specialty, intended use, lifecycle implication, and what kind of evidence or quality controls a responsible reader should consider.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Device AI can be tracked with public evidence instead of vague market claims.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: MedTech regulatory, QMS, clinical evaluation, software validation, and product teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Build a device-intelligence table with specialty, intended use, clearance date, and lifecycle implication.

visual.title: Device intelligence as a map
visual.footer: A list can become intelligence only when it is structured for action.

signals.claimBoundary: Do not infer device safety, performance, or market share beyond the public FDA list.

signals.title: Good machine-learning practice is moving into quality language.
signals.cardFact: FDA's SaMD AI materials keep AI/ML medical-device software tied to lifecycle, transparency, change-control, monitoring, and good machine-learning-practice concepts.
signals.whyItMatters: AI quality is becoming less about novelty and more about traceable intended use, data, validation, transparency, monitoring, and change records.
signals.sourceTitle: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Software as a Medical Device
signals.expandedSummary: The FDA SaMD AI topic page supports a quality-system reading of AI. The useful W25 signal is that good machine-learning practice and lifecycle thinking should become record structures, not presentation language. iFeed can turn this into a reusable QMS lens: purpose, data, training context, validation, transparency, monitoring, update logic, and change decision.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: AI tools become risky when update logic and evidence boundaries are not controlled.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: QMS, software validation, regulatory, product, clinical safety, and post-market teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Create an AI software control record with intended use, dataset, validation, monitoring, and change trigger.

visual.title: AI quality follows the lifecycle
visual.footer: Quality language becomes useful when it creates reviewable records.

signals.claimBoundary: This is a governance/readiness interpretation, not a claim that one FDA topic page creates a new W25 obligation.

signals.title: Clinical AI needs reviewable medical-product evidence.
signals.cardFact: FDA's AI and medical products page treats AI as a cross-product issue spanning devices, drugs, biologics, evidence, oversight, and research.
signals.whyItMatters: AI in medical products and clinical workflows needs a visible evidence trail before it can become trusted operating infrastructure.
signals.sourceType: FDA science and research topic page
signals.expandedSummary: FDA's AI and medical products page is not a single clinical-trial rule, but it is an important operating signal. AI is no longer a narrow software sidebar. It sits across medical products, clinical evidence, and oversight. iFeed should use this to frame clinical AI as evidence work: source, model purpose, workflow point, human review, decision boundary, and audit trail.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Clinical AI can affect product evidence and decisions before the record is ready.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: Clinical operations, medical product teams, regulatory strategy, QA, data science, and safety.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Map every AI use to a workflow point, reviewer, decision boundary, and retained evidence record.

visual.title: Clinical AI as evidence work
visual.footer: If AI changes a decision, the record must show how.

signals.claimBoundary: Do not claim a trial-specific regulatory change from this source without a trial-specific guidance source.

signals.title: EU HTA is becoming a planning system, not a late dossier step.
signals.cardFact: The European Commission HTA page anchors joint clinical assessment and joint scientific consultation as part of the EU health technology assessment operating environment.
signals.whyItMatters: Evidence planning has to connect regulatory, clinical, statistical, HTA, and payer questions before late-stage submission pressure begins.
signals.expandedSummary: The European Commission HTA source supports a market-access signal: value evidence is moving upstream. iFeed should frame HTA not as a final dossier activity but as a planning system that shapes what evidence is generated, how it is explained, and how regulatory and payer questions are separated. This is especially relevant to iFeed's market research and service readiness layers.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Approval evidence and access evidence can ask different questions.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: Market access, regulatory strategy, clinical development, health economics, and statistics.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Add HTA question, comparator, outcome, and uncertainty fields to evidence-planning records.

visual.title: Access starts upstream
visual.footer: Access is designed before the dossier hardens.

signals.claimBoundary: Do not claim reimbursement outcome, payer decision, or product-specific value from this source.

signals.title: Health data space is becoming readiness infrastructure.
signals.cardFact: The European Health Data Space frames health data access, use, and governance as an EU-level infrastructure question.
signals.whyItMatters: AI, research, quality systems, and market intelligence all depend on provenance, access rights, reuse logic, and governance controls.
signals.sourceType: European Commission digital health policy page
signals.expandedSummary: The European Health Data Space source gives W25 a healthcare infrastructure signal. The practical point for iFeed is that health data is becoming an operating layer. AI readiness, research evidence, QMS controls, and market intelligence all need clarity around data provenance, access, secondary use, governance, and accountability. This belongs in both the public research layer and the internal cockpit model.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Data access and reuse rules determine what AI, research, and evidence workflows can safely do.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: Digital health, data governance, research, quality, privacy, and platform teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Create a health-data readiness checklist: source, access right, purpose, reuse limit, and governance owner.

visual.title: Data becomes infrastructure
visual.footer: Intelligence depends on governed data movement.

signals.claimBoundary: Do not convert general EHDS policy into organization-specific operational claims without implementation-level evidence.

signals.title: Drug-development AI needs submission-grade operating records.
signals.cardFact: FDA CDER's AI in drug development page shows AI activity becoming an operating and submission-readiness question for drug development teams.
signals.whyItMatters: AI use in drug development needs enough context to explain purpose, data, method, validation rationale, human review, and where it affects decisions.
signals.expandedSummary: FDA CDER's AI in drug development page supports a biopharma AI signal for W25. The iFeed reading is that AI use in development is not just an innovation story. It needs submission-grade operating records: model purpose, input data, output role, validation rationale, human review, limitation, and decision impact. This is a natural bridge between iFeed Signals, AI healthcare, and future academy content.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Drug-development AI may influence evidence, decisions, or submission narratives.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: R&D, clinical development, regulatory strategy, data science, quality, and statistics.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: For each AI use, record purpose, data, output role, reviewer, limitation, and submission relevance.

visual.title: AI needs submission context
visual.footer: Innovation becomes durable when the record can travel.

signals.claimBoundary: Do not claim FDA endorsement of any specific AI approach unless the source says so.

signals.title: Public-source regulation needs a claim boundary before commentary.
signals.cardFact: A regulatory signal is stronger when the public fact, the iFeed interpretation, and the non-legal-advice boundary are separate fields.
signals.whyItMatters: Regulatory commentary can overclaim quickly unless each source-backed statement keeps evidence, interpretation, and action boundary apart.
signals.sourceTitle: Draft Commission guidelines on classification of high-risk AI systems
signals.expandedSummary: This signal deliberately uses the same high-risk AI source as Signal 1 for a different purpose: method control. W25 is not only about what changed in the market. It is also about how iFeed should read regulatory movement. The Signal Cockpit should keep source fact, source class, iFeed interpretation, and claim boundary as separate fields. That is how a public digest can stay useful without turning into unbounded advice.

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Regulatory intelligence needs traceability before it needs confident commentary.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: Editorial, regulatory, quality, legal review, and source verification teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Use four fields for each regulatory item: source fact, source class, iFeed view, and boundary.

visual.title: Source before commentary
visual.footer: Good intelligence survives review because the source is visible.

signals.claimBoundary: This method signal duplicates the Signal 1 source; final editorial review should decide whether both should remain in the public issue.

signals.title: Clinical trial quality is becoming an operating-system requirement.
signals.cardFact: FDA ties clinical-trial oversight to federal law, GCP regulations, participant protection, robust evidence, and quality clinical evidence.
signals.whyItMatters: Trial modernization, AI use, and digital workflows still need to resolve into participant protection, protocol intent, source data, oversight, essential records, evidence quality, and quality management.
signals.sourceTitle: Clinical Trials and Human Subject Protection
signals.sourceType: FDA clinical trials and GCP page
signals.expandedSummary: The FDA clinical-trials and human-subject-protection page gives W25 a clinical-trial quality anchor. FDA describes oversight around trial design, conduct, analysis, reporting, participant protection, GCP regulations, and evidence quality. The iFeed interpretation is that modernization is not a reason to loosen the operating file. Whether teams are dealing with AI, digital tools, decentralized elements, or adaptive operations, the useful question remains: what record proves participant protection, protocol intent, source truth, oversight, training, deviation handling, and quality management?

tiles.label: Why it matters
tiles.text: Modern trials still need protected participants, reliable records, and evidence that can be inspected, explained, and trusted.

tiles.label: Owner
tiles.text: Clinical operations, sponsor oversight, QA, data management, sites, and trial technology teams.

tiles.label: Monday move
tiles.text: Map each trial innovation to participant-protection, protocol, source, oversight, essential-record, and QMS evidence.

visual.title: Clinical quality needs an operating file
visual.footer: Modernization is strongest when participant protection and evidence quality become easier to verify.

signals.claimBoundary: Do not claim FDA endorsement of a specific trial technology or AI approach from this general page; use it as a clinical-trial quality and GCP oversight anchor.
